interpretation: The Union sacred political order at its Civil War peak demonstrates high sacred-political-order strength animated by anti-sacrificial restraint: Lincoln's Gettysburg framing directs the dead soldiers' sacrifice toward democratic re-founding rather than collective immortality through enemy annihilation, while the Second Inaugural — delivered at the moment of military victory — refuses malice and calls for charity toward the defeated. This is a hybrid case because the order required enormous sacrifice to preserve itself, yet its leading symbolic architect explicitly refused to convert that sacrifice into sacral hatred of the enemy. The result is a sacred political order that achieved high strength through bounded sacrifice, distinguishing it from escalatory cases like Nazi Germany or Khmer Rouge.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin but directly on point. Reconstruction's subsequent collapse complicates the anti-sacrificial reading; score applies to the order's peak Lincoln-era formulation.

mechanismSummary: At the moment of maximum symbolic power (military victory), Lincoln redirected the war's moral energy away from enemy-destruction and toward healing, refusing the escalatory logic available to him — a deliberate anti-sacrificial framing that distinguishes the Union case from pathological comparators.

interpretation: The British Empire to Commonwealth transition scores high on corrigibility through its demonstration of symbolic transformation from above: the Balfour Declaration reframed hierarchical subordination as equal voluntary association, and the Statute of Westminster legally implemented this reframing by requiring Dominion consent for shared constitutional changes. This is the absorption-transformation archetype — an imperial order that avoided frozen-pathology by redesigning its own symbolic architecture in response to the changed conditions produced by World War I and rising Dominion nationalism. The transformation was incomplete (decolonization of non-Dominion territories remained partial and violent), but the symbolic redesign itself demonstrates a corrigibility mechanism: the ability to fundamentally revise the order's constitutive hierarchy without sacrificial escalation against internal challengers.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin; passages are the constitutive legal moments of the transformation but require expansion to include decolonization history and Commonwealth operational record.

mechanismSummary: The British imperial order redesigned its constitutive symbolic architecture from hierarchical subordination to equal voluntary association, converting the Crown from instrument of control to symbol of free association — demonstrating corrigibility through self-revision rather than sacrificial defense of the existing order.

interpretation: The Canadian sacred political order scores high on corrigibility through the Charter's built-in self-correction mechanism: Section 1 makes every rights guarantee provisional on demonstrable justification in a free and democratic society, embedding proportionality review as a permanent institutional corrective. Section 15's equality guarantee pairs with this to prevent the sacred political order from entrenching hierarchy through sacred-enemy or in-group/out-group distinctions. The result is a constitutional order that maintains high symbolic strength while preserving the institutional capacity to correct pathological tendencies — the structural inverse of the high-pathology sacrificial-escalation cases.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin but passages are directly constitutive. The notwithstanding clause (s.33) and its use by Quebec complicates a maximum corrigibility score. Score reflects the design intent of the order, not every operational episode.

mechanismSummary: Section 1's proportionality review requirement institutionalizes order self-correction, preventing rights violations from becoming entrenched — a constitutional-containment mechanism that structurally opposes sacrificial escalation.

interpretation: The Confederacy's founding documents establish a sacred political order of high initial strength: the Constitution claims divine guidance for permanent federal sovereignty, while Stephens explicitly codes racial hierarchy as the order's 'corner-stone' — an immutable natural truth rather than a political arrangement subject to revision. This sacralization of racial hierarchy made the order incorrigible by design, producing the rigidity that contributed to collapse when military defeat removed the institutional basis for sustaining the claim.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain is thin but passages are directly on point and verified. Score reflects high sacred-political-order strength at founding; collapse mechanism requires separate chain expansion.

mechanismSummary: The Confederate sacred political order achieved high founding strength by sacralizing racial hierarchy as natural truth, but its institutional base collapsed under military defeat without the corrigibility needed to adapt, producing terminal fragmentation.

interpretation: The European Union sacred political order demonstrates high corrigibility through its constitutive pluralist architecture: the TEU's 'ever closer union' frames integration as an open-ended process rather than a sacred destination, and its grounding in liberty, democracy, and rule of law provides normative self-correction mechanisms. Unlike the high-pathology cases, the EU's sacred political order cannot generate a sacred enemy from within its own structure — its legitimacy depends on multi-state consensus and legal adjudication. Democratic-backsliding episodes (Hungary, Poland) and Brexit test this corrigibility but also activate the order's correction mechanisms (Article 7, infringement procedures), supporting rather than undermining the corrigibility score.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin. Founding document passages establish the design intent; operational corrigibility requires expansion. Democratic-backsliding episodes introduce uncertainty.

mechanismSummary: The EU's multi-state, open-ended integration architecture structurally opposes sacred-enemy escalation and sacrificial demands, producing high corrigibility through consensus-building and legal adjudication rather than symbolic unification around a common threat.

interpretation: The Gandhian sacred political order achieves moderate-high symbolic strength through a theoretically novel route: rather than sacralizing national identity through sacred-enemy construction or blood-sacrifice demands, Gandhi's doctrine redirects sacrifice entirely to self-suffering. Body-force (violence against the enemy) is explicitly rejected as inferior to soul-force (accepting penalty for truth-bearing resistance). This anti-sacrificial logic gives the sacred political order high corrigibility — the movement cannot generate internal purges or sacrificial escalation from its own constitutive principles — while achieving independence through collective action. The clearest theoretical countercase to the pathological sacrificial-escalation gold cases.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin; theoretical founding texts only. Operational episodes and partition violence complicate the score.

mechanismSummary: Gandhi's satyagraha doctrine explicitly substitutes self-suffering for enemy-directed violence, achieving political transformation through soul-force rather than body-force — a constitutive anti-sacrificial restraint that structurally prevents sacred-enemy escalation.

interpretation: The Imperial Rescripts institutionalized a maximally strong sacred political order in which the kokutai (national polity) organized identity, legitimacy, obligation, memory, and sacrifice around the Emperor as a divine collective body. Individual survival was explicitly subordinated to imperial survival, and death in service of the Emperor was framed as the highest moral fulfillment.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Structural alignment with the theory is strong; chain is a minimal stub requiring expansion with wartime sources and secondary scholarship before scholarly use.

mechanismSummary: The imperial sacred political order represented the national body as divinely eternal; individual death in service of that body was encoded as participation in its immortality, making sacrifice the highest moral act.

interpretation: The Kokutai no Hongi defined corrigibility-elimination as an ideological positive: loyalty requires 'casting ourselves aside,' making individual judgment an act of disloyalty and Western individualism a contaminant to be overcome. The sole legitimate mode of existence for subjects was unconditional service to the Emperor — 'the sole Way in which we subjects may live.' This is not merely a practical constraint but a constitutive ontological claim: individual selfhood outside imperial service is framed as non-existence. Combined with the 1941 war rescript's existential framing of the Pacific War, the sacred political order produced a corrigibility floor of near-zero: no institutional mechanism existed for questioning the war's direction without transgressing the fundamental definition of what it meant to 'live' as a Japanese subject.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Kokutai no Hongi text directly supports the corrigibility-elimination interpretation: the equation of loyalty with self-erasure and the framing of Western individualism as contamination make the claim textually grounded. War rescript adds the sacred-enemy dimension. Confidence capped: operational documentation of how these ideological prescriptions functioned in practice requires chain expansion.

mechanismSummary: The Kokutai no Hongi defined individual selfhood outside imperial service as non-existence, eliminating the basis for legitimate dissent; the war rescript activated sacred-enemy escalation by framing the war as defense of national existence — together producing minimum corrigibility.

interpretation: The DK sacred political order explicitly encoded the collective revolutionary body as sacred and eternal, demanding the sacrifice of children and family members by the hundreds of thousands. The national anthem literalized blood sacrifice as the constitutive act of the nation. This is a maximum collective-immortality case: the individual is absorbed into the collective body and their death is framed as the body's self-renewal.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Chain is thin but passages are directly on point. Score is defensible; pending chain expansion.

mechanismSummary: The Angkar represented the collective revolutionary body as a sacred entity whose survival transcended individual life; the Constitution and anthem explicitly frame the sacrifice of family and blood as constitutive of national rebirth.

interpretation: The Maoist sacred political order established 'the people' as an eternal collective body whose continuation gave meaning to individual death. Mao's explicit contrast — death for the people is weightier than Mount Tai, death for exploiters is lighter than a feather — encodes a graded hierarchy of sacrifice in which only death that serves collective immortality is meaningful. Selflessness is not merely praised but structurally required.

confidence.label: moderate-high

mechanismSummary: The Maoist sacred political order represented 'the People' as a transcendent collective body; Mao's canonical texts explicitly coded death-for-the-people as the supreme form of human meaning, structurally demanding individual self-abnegation.

interpretation: The draft evidence chain supports a preliminary reading of the Nazi Volksgemeinschaft as an imagined collective body whose sacred survival justified individual sacrifice.

The draft chain identifies a totalizing national sacred political order that organizes belonging, legitimacy, memory, obligation, and sacrifice.
interpretation: The Nazi Volksgemeinschaft is provisionally coded as a very strong sacred political order, while remaining explicitly draft pending verified source passages and broader evidence.

interpretation: The Nazi sacred political order scores at minimum corrigibility: the Wehrmacht oath's 'unconditional obedience' and readiness to die for the oath eliminated institutional capacity for legitimate dissent within the armed forces by converting legal obligation from constitutional duty to personal fealty to the Führer; simultaneously, the Law Against Formation of New Parties legally extinguished the pluralist competition that enables order self-correction. Together these two legal acts in 1933–1934 completed the structural elimination of corrigibility: no institutional actor could legitimately challenge the sacred political order's direction without violating a personal sacred oath or committing a criminal act. This is the corrigibility-elimination that distinguishes Nazi Germany from the restrained-reordering cases.

confidence.label: high
confidence.rationale: Claim derives from two short, legally unambiguous texts verified against authoritative sources. The corrigibility interpretation is direct: unconditional-obedience oath + single-party law = elimination of legitimate dissent channels. High confidence relative to most chain interpretations.

mechanismSummary: Personal loyalty oath replacing constitutional duty + legal single-party monopoly simultaneously eliminated all institutional corrigibility mechanisms, converting legitimate dissent into personal betrayal and criminal act.

interpretation: North Korea presents the clearest constitutional case of frozen-pathology in the dataset: the state is definitionally the application of the eternal leaders' Juche ideas, and the entire population is constitutionally bound to uphold those eternal leaders and carry their ideas forward unchanged. This produces maximum sacred-political-order strength through total institutional monopoly while eliminating corrigibility by constitutional design — the sacred political order cannot correct itself because correction is constitutionally prohibited as a violation of the eternal leaders' mandate. The mechanism is frozen-pathology rather than active sacrificial escalation: the order is not generating new enemies but is locked in a permanent defensive posture that prevents adaptation.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Constitutional text supports the interpretation clearly, but confidence is capped by translation-chain uncertainty and the absence of operational documentation. Source access limitations documented in source-pack.

mechanismSummary: The DPRK sacred political order constitutionally encodes the eternal leaders' ideas as the unchangeable foundation of the state, eliminating corrigibility by design and locking the order in permanent Juche ideology regardless of changing conditions.

interpretation: The post-9/11 US sacred political order demonstrates a hybrid pattern: high sacred-political-order strength achieved through sacred-enemy escalation rather than constitutional-containment. The AUMF's unlimited force authorization and Bush's globally totalized binary ('with us or with the terrorists') temporarily maximized the sacred political order's strength by sacralizing national identity through absolute external threat. This distinguishes post-9/11 from the Vietnam aftermath (which produced constitutional-containment via the WPR) and from the gold high-pathology cases (where sacred-enemy escalation targeted internal populations). The 'forever war' trajectory shows the instability of strength-through-sacred-enemy — the enemy definition expanded indefinitely, producing institutional fragmentation rather than consolidation.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin; passages establish the founding pattern but trajectory scoring requires expansion. Hybrid case complexity adds uncertainty.

mechanismSummary: 9/11 shock was channeled into a globally totalized sacred-enemy binary through unlimited congressional force authorization and presidential framing, temporarily maximizing sacred-political-order strength at the cost of institutional constraint and long-term legitimacy.

interpretation: The Basic Law's inviolable human dignity provision (Art. 1 GG) and eternity clause (Art. 79(3) GG) are direct constitutional embodiments of high corrigibility: the postwar sacred political order structurally limits its own capacity for pathological escalation by placing individual dignity beyond collective override.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Interpretation is well-grounded in the constitutional text and contrasts cleanly with the Nazi Germany sacrificial-escalation case. Chain is currently a minimal stub.

mechanismSummary: The founding constitutional document embeds human dignity as inviolable and unamendable, directly blocking the collective-body logic that characterized the preceding pathological order.

interpretation: Article 20's resistance clause (Art. 20(4) GG) is the constitutive memory-driven-restraint provision of the postwar German sacred political order: the framers converted the Weimar experience of legal revolution — Hitler's use of enabling legislation to abolish parliamentary democracy through formally legal means — into a positive constitutional right to resist abolition of the democratic order. Combined with Art. 79(3)'s protection of Art. 20 as an eternity clause, this creates a two-layer corrigibility architecture: Art. 20 defines the protected democratic-social order; Art. 79(3) makes it unamendable; Art. 20(4) arms citizens to defend it when all other remedies fail. The memory of the pathological order is thus encoded as a constitutional right of resistance against its repetition — the clearest example of memory-driven-restraint in the dataset.

confidence.label: high
confidence.rationale: Text verified against official translation; scholarly consensus on framers' intent is clear (Weimar-lesson design); the Art. 20(4) / Art. 79(3) architectural relationship is analytically tight. Confidence capped: Art. 20(4) was added in 1968, not 1949, and the right has never been formally invoked, raising questions about its operative force.

mechanismSummary: Art. 20(1)+(4) converts the Weimar trauma of legal revolution into a constitutional right of resistance, encoding the memory of pathological collapse as a positive corrective mechanism — the democratic order cannot be legally abolished, and citizens are constitutionally empowered to prevent its abolition by other means.

interpretation: The revolutionary sacred political order achieved maximum sacred-political-order strength by vesting all legitimate authority exclusively in the Nation, while Robespierre's virtue-terror synthesis sacralized the revolutionary order itself as the embodiment of virtue. This produced a configuration in which the sacred political order could not be corrected without betraying the revolution — critique became treason, and terror became a moral obligation.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Passages are well-attested canonical texts. The mechanism is well-supported for the Terror phase. Confidence capped because the claim applies primarily to 1793–1794.

mechanismSummary: The Declaration of Rights invested the Nation with absolute sovereignty while Robespierre fused virtue and terror — making the revolutionary order itself sacred and directing violence at internal enemies whose elimination was framed as moral necessity.

interpretation: The Soviet communist sacred political order achieved maximum sacred-political-order strength at its Stalinist peak by fusing class-struggle eschatology (proletarians have a world to win) with institutional monopoly (the CPSU as nucleus of all Soviet life). This is a hybrid case: the order's founding logic demanded sacrifice toward collective immortality through historical inevitability, and its constitutional structure eliminated the institutional corrigibility needed to correct that demand. The result is a sacred political order of high initial strength that could not self-correct, producing the frozen-pathology trajectory observed in the terminal phase (see soviet-union-collapse). The two passages bookend this arc: the Manifesto's world-historical call for total transformation, and the Brezhnev Constitution's legalization of the party's unchallengeability.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin but passages are definitional. Requires expansion from founding ideology to institutional practice to complete the arc. Hybrid complexity acknowledged.

mechanismSummary: The Soviet sacred political order fused world-historical eschatology with vanguard institutional monopoly, demanding sacrifice in service of collective communist immortality — a structure that produced high initial strength but no corrigibility, making frozen-pathology the terminal outcome.

interpretation: The Soviet sacred political order had historically high strength (Marxist-Leninist ideology organizing identity, legitimacy, obligation, and sacrifice across a vast institutional apparatus), but by 1985-1991 that strength had eroded to the point where the order could not survive sustained crisis pressure. Gorbachev's admission of systemic failure and the formal dissolution of the USSR mark the terminal phase of legitimacy collapse: the sacred political order could neither sustain its founding claims nor suppress the centrifugal forces its rigidity had generated.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Legitimacy-collapse reading is well-supported by the dissolution evidence; the precise trajectory from strength to collapse requires fuller periodization and secondary source corroboration.

mechanismSummary: The Soviet sacred political order's inability to acknowledge systemic failure until too late — a decades-long suppression of correction — depleted the legitimacy of its founding claims, and the reform process that might have saved the order instead revealed and accelerated its disintegration.

interpretation: The Soviet order's corrigibility failure is documented by two symmetrical failures bracketing the reform period: Gorbachev's 1987 CC Plenum diagnosis shows the order's late-stage awareness that stagnation had accumulated ('difficulties and unsolved problems began to pile up'), but the 'no other way' framing reveals correction as a last-resort imperative rather than a functioning institutional mechanism — the order could acknowledge failure but had no path to correction at scale. The GKChP's 1991 declaration that reform 'has reached a dead end' shows that when correction was finally attempted, the order's own institutional actors mobilized to abort it. This two-sided failure — correction too late and correction suppressed from within — is the characteristic signature of minimal corrigibility: the system contained neither the early-warning mechanisms nor the institutional consensus required to course-correct before terminal crisis.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Both passage texts are verified. The interpretation extends the existing legitimacy-collapse reading by documenting the corrigibility dimension: the mechanisms available for self-correction were insufficient and ultimately self-defeating. Confidence below 0.80 because corrigibility = 1 (minimum) requires ruling out alternative mechanisms — factionalism did provide some channel for contestation, and Gorbachev's reforms themselves constituted a corrigibility attempt.

mechanismSummary: The order's inability to correct stagnation before crisis and the counter-coup blocking correction in 1991 together demonstrate institutional fragmentation severe enough to prevent effective corrigibility from either direction.

interpretation: Switzerland scores high on corrigibility through its constitutional inversion of the sacrificial symbolic logic: the Preamble defines collective strength as the well-being of the weakest rather than the sacrifice of the weak for the strong, while Article 2 pluralizes the order's aims (welfare, diversity, sustainability, peaceful order) in ways that structurally resist sacred-enemy simplification. The federalist and direct-democracy mechanisms compound this: power is constitutionally distributed across cantons and referenda, preventing symbolic concentration that enables sacrificial escalation. This is the structural countercase to the frozen-pathology trajectory — the Swiss order achieves stability through pluralist consensus rather than through vanguard monopoly.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain thin; passages are directly constitutive but require expansion to direct-democracy practices and federalism. Historical exclusion of women's suffrage (until 1971 federally) complicates maximum corrigibility score.

mechanismSummary: Constitutional definition of strength-as-welfare-of-weakest plus pluralized aims and federalist/direct-democracy structures prevent symbolic concentration and sacred-enemy escalation, producing high corrigibility through consensus rather than vanguard monopoly.

interpretation: The post-Vietnam US sacred political order exhibited moderate corrigibility: the War Powers Resolution institutionalized congressional oversight as a correction mechanism against unilateral executive war-making, and Carter's public acknowledgment of national crisis represented a order willingness to receive criticism rather than deflect it through sacrificial mobilization. This contrasts with Imperial Japan and Nazi Germany, where equivalent crises intensified sacrificial demands. Corrigibility was partial and contested — Reagan's explicit campaign to overcome the Vietnam syndrome within a decade shows the correction was not deeply institutionalized.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Structural alignment with corrigibility is supported by the legislative and rhetorical evidence, but the subsequent rollback complicates the score. Minimal stub requiring expansion.

mechanismSummary: The collective memory of Vietnam failure — institutionalized as the War Powers Resolution and expressed in Carter's crisis rhetoric — restrained further sacrificial escalation and enabled a period of constrained reordering rather than pathological self-preservation.

interpretation: The post-Vietnam US sacred political order exhibits moderate and declining strength during the 1973–1984 period covered by the chain: War Powers § 5(b)'s automatic withdrawal mechanism encodes Congress's assessment that the President alone could not be trusted to maintain symbolic-order coherence under crisis pressure, implying the founding legitimacy framework (executive war authority, Cold War consensus) had significantly eroded; Carter's crisis diagnosis (passage-002) names sacred-political-order-strength erosion explicitly as the central national problem; and Weinberger's explicit return to pre-engagement public consent-seeking (passage-004) shows the order reconstructing its authority basis around democratic accountability rather than imperial prerogative. Score of 3 reflects a sacred political order that retains structural coherence (democratic institutions, legal framework) but whose legitimacy claims have been substantially contested and its sacrifice demands constrained by collective memory.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: The sacred-political-order-strength reading is strongly supported by the Carter passage (explicit crisis diagnosis) and consistent with the WPR and Weinberger passages. Score of 3 is plausible but contested: the Cold War consensus partially survived Vietnam, and Reagan's successful rearmament campaign by 1983 suggests symbolic-order recovery was faster than a mid-period reading implies.

mechanismSummary: The Vietnam memory restrained order expansion by requiring legal authorization and public consent before military deployment, reducing the available repertoire of sacrificial mobilization and constraining executive unilateralism.

interpretation: The Western Roman sacred political order had achieved maximum strength at its height through civic sacrifice, legal order, and the myth of eternal Rome. By the late fourth century, contemporary witnesses diagnosed decay: Vegetius saw military discipline collapse under peacetime complacency; Augustine, writing after the Sack of Rome (410), analyzed how Roman civic virtue had been animated by love of glory for the earthly city rather than transcendent goods — a motivation that could not sustain the sacred political order when its material conditions failed. This is a collapse case driven by institutional fragmentation rather than sacrificial escalation.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Chain is thin; passages are diagnostic rather than constitutive. Score reflects the order's peak strength, not its terminal state. Pending chain expansion.

mechanismSummary: Long peace eroded the military discipline sustaining the sacred political order; Augustine's analysis suggests the order's civic sacrifice logic was grounded in glory-seeking for the earthly city rather than transcendent goods, making it unable to renew itself as material conditions deteriorated.

interpretation: At the outbreak of World War I, multiple belligerent sacred political orders achieved maximum mobilizational strength by invoking the nation as a sacred collective body that dissolved all internal distinctions. Kaiser Wilhelm's dissolution of party divisions and Poincaré's 'sacred union' both demonstrate the mechanism: the sacred political order expands to absorb all individuals into a single sacrificial body at the moment of perceived existential threat.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Passages are well-attested verified texts. The mechanism is well-supported for the mobilization moment. Confidence capped given multi-nation unit of analysis.

mechanismSummary: At war outbreak, national sacred political orders in Germany and France suppressed all internal distinctions and sacralized the nation as a collective body demanding total sacrifice — Kaiser Wilhelm recognized 'only Germans,' Poincaré invoked 'sacred union.'
